intain our assumption for a 363.3-million-share issuance via private placement to occur
in mid-2025 at a price of VND100,000/share, implying expected total proceeds of VND36.3tn
(USD1.5bn).
• Risks to our positive outlook: (1) Capital raising does not materialize as expected; (2) lower-
than-expected credit growth; (3) higher-than-expected NPL formation; (4) risks arising from
participation in the rehabilitation of a distressed credit institution (DCI).
We expect NIM to remain flat QoQ in Q4 2024 and begin to improve in 2025F. VCB reported a
relatively weak NIM in Q3 2024, which we partly attribute to preferential lending rates amid
resilient credit growth in the quarter. In Q4 2024, we expect to maintain a low lending rate to
boost credit growth, reaching 14% in FY2024. Stronger credit growth in Q4 could be supported by
(i) seasonal factors and (ii) the disbursement of major projects in public expenditures and
Duy Nguyen, PhD
infrastructure with longer durations. For FY2025, we expect NIM will improve by 19 bps to 3.21%

6 The VAMC acquired approximately VND 310,000 billion (equivalent to around USD$13.5 billion) in NPLs (more than 26,000 bad
loans) by the end of 2017, financed by the issuance of VAMC special (interest-free) bonds. Most of the non-performing assets
were transferred from commercial banks to the VAMC at close to book value.
7 In 2014, deposit insurance was VND 50 million per deposit (about USD $2,000 equivalent).
8 https://english.luatvietnam.vn/resolution-no-42-2017-qh14-dated-june-21-2017-of-the-national-assembly-on-the-

8. The implementation of Basel II standards, formalized through Circular No. 41/2016, was a crucial
reform aimed at enhancing risk management and capital adequacy of the banking sector. Basel II
introduced stricter capital adequacy, requiring banks to maintain a minimum CAR of 8 percent,
calculated based on credit, market, and operational risks. This fram

averaging 14 percent annually over the past five years - even during the COVID-19 pandemic, (Figure
2).
Restructuring of the banking sector post-2012 banking crisis
3. The 2012 banking crisis was triggered by a surge in non-performing loans (NPLs), prompting the
Government to issue Decision 254, which outlined a three-year scheme to restructure credit
institutions. This initiative led to notable progress in addressing system liquidity, strengthened
supervision and regulation, sector consolidation, and NPL resolution. Subsequently, over a dozen
troubled banks2 were either merged or brought under direct control by the State Bank of Viet Nam
(SBV): the number of domestic commercial banks was reduced to 35 from 52.

ice for end-2025 — a residual
income method and target P/B valuation — with a 50% weighting assigned for each method.
In this Update Report, we raise our target price (TP) for VCB by 3.9% to VND114,300/share and
maintain a BUY rating. Our higher TP is mainly due to the positive impact from rolling our TP
horizon forward to end-2025. This is partly offset by a 10.9% aggregate decrease in our 2024-
2028F NPAT-MI (with respective changes of -6.6%/-14.1%/-14.4%/-10.7%/-8.8% in
2024/25/26/27/28F). Additionally, we maintain our target P/B of 3.2x.
Our lower 2024-2028F NPAT-MI is primarily due to (1) a 6.9% aggregate decrease in NII following
a downward revision in our average NIM assumption from 3.56% to 3.31%, (2) an 11.4% aggregate
decrease in NFI (including gains from FX trading) driven by the slower-than-expected recovery
of banca activities. These are partly offset by a 3.2% aggregate decrease in operating expenses.
We continue to model for VCB’s LLR to converge within a band centered by the peer median at
the end of the explicit forecast period under the residual income method — even if the reality in
future years does not conform with this view — to make valuation models comparabl

valuation_methodology.chunk:
ice for end-2025 — a residual
income method and target P/B valuation — with a 50% weighting assigned for each method.
In this Update Report, we raise our target price (TP) for VCB by 3.9% to VND114,300/share and
maintain a BUY rating. Our higher TP is mainly due to the positive impact from rolling our TP
horizon forward to end-2025. This is partly offset by a 10.9% aggregate decrease in our 2024-
2028F NPAT-MI (with respective changes of -6.6%/-14.1%/-14.4%/-10.7%/-8.8% in
2024/25/26/27/28F). Additionally, we maintain our target P/B of 3.2x.
Our lower 2024-2028F NPAT-MI is primarily due to (1) a 6.9% aggregate decrease in NII following
a downward revision in our average NIM assumption from 3.56% to 3.31%, (2) an 11.4% aggregate
decrease in NFI (including gains from FX trading) driven by the slower-than-expected recovery
of banca activities. These are partly offset by a 3.2% aggregate decrease in operating expenses.
We continue to model for VCB’s LLR to converge within a band centered by the peer median at
the end of the explicit forecast period under the residual income method — even if the reality in
future years does not conform with this view — to make valuation models comparabl
